result: Lantern light spills across the crooked stones, coaxing sleepy vendors awake. Tonight I wander through the old riverside market, curious about how memory tastes when dipped in steam and spice. A brass kettle whistles; its breath carries peppermint, charred citrus, faint tobacco. The aromas mingle, forming a language older than my ancestors’ names. I imagine conversations between those scents, gossiping about lovers, monsoons, unfinished songs. Nearby, a violinist tunes her cracked instrument. Each note stumbles, then stands straighter, like a child learning courage. I offer her a coin, and she repays me with a waltz so fragile it might splinter in cold air. Instead, the melody loops around my wrists, weaving bracelets of echo. Further on, a baker flips sesame pancakes. He tells me stories about dough rising like revolution, fierce yet patient. I bite into one and the crust crackles, revealing sweetness hiding behind smoke. Suddenly I remember the atlas I kept as a teenager, dog-eared, heavy with circles around ports I swore to visit. Many remain untouched, but nights like this shrink the distance between promise and presence. I pocket a stray plum blossom, pressing it between receipts as proof that wonder can be archived. When rain begins, the market doesn’t scatter. It simply adjusts, tilting awnings like umbrellas, inviting the sky to taste our feast. I walk home soaked, humming the violinist’s waltz, feeling the bracelets of echo tighten, pulse, disappear. In their place remains a quiet assurance that tomorrow will arrive, lantern-lit, fragrant, full of unwritten.
